questions.questionText: Glycolysis conceptually occurs in which subcellular compartment?
questions.explanation: The enzymes of the glycolytic pathway are localized in the cytoplasm.

questions.questionText: The rate-limiting and committed step of glycolysis is catalyzed by:
questions.explanation: PFK-1 converts fructose-6-P to fructose-1,6-bisP and is tightly regulated.

questions.questionText: In anaerobic conditions, pyruvate is converted to lactate to regenerate:
questions.explanation: Lactate dehydrogenase uses NADH to reduce pyruvate, regenerating NAD+ for glycolysis to continue.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme is unique to gluconeogenesis?
questions.explanation: PEPCK circumvents the irreversible pyruvate kinase step of glycolysis.

questions.questionText: The major site for gluconeogenesis is the:
questions.explanation: The liver uses non-carbohydrate precursors to maintain blood glucose.

questions.questionText: The key regulatory enzyme of glycogenesis (glycogen synthesis) is:
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase adds glucose residues via alpha(1->4) linkages.

questions.questionText: Insulin promotes which of the following processes?
questions.explanation: Insulin is an anabolic hormone that promotes storage of glucose as glycogen.

questions.questionText: The pentose phosphate pathway is important for generating ribose-5-phosphate and:
questions.explanation: NADPH is needed for reductive biosynthesis (e.g., fatty acids) and antioxidant defense.

questions.questionText: A deficiency of Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase (G6PD) predominantly affects:
questions.explanation: RBCs rely exclusively on the PPP to generate NADPH to protect against oxidative stress.

questions.questionText: Galactosemia is most commonly caused by a deficiency in:
questions.explanation: Classic galactosemia leads to accumulation of toxic galactose-1-phosphate.

questions.questionText: In Carbohydrate Metabolism, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Biochemistry texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Biochemistry references for Carbohydrate Metabolism. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Carbohydrate Metabolism.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Carbohydrate Metabolism scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Biochemistry. Chapter: Carbohydrate Metabolism.
